rows.question: Why did you leave Soho House?
rows.answer:
After about two and a half years I'd reached the point where the role wasn't teaching me anything new. The experience was genuinely strong — high service standards, a demanding environment, daily work with Salesforce to manage member context — but toward the end I was executing well on something I'd already mastered.

What was pulling me was the technology and systems side of the industry. I'd seen first-hand how a badly handled PMS migration can destabilise a whole operation, and I wanted to be closer to that — implementation, adoption, the bridge between product and real operations. Soho House wasn't going to offer that path, so I made a deliberate move toward it.

No conflict, no abrupt exit — just a clear sense that the next thing I needed to learn wasn't going to come from staying.
rows.reason: The answer provides the established grounded reason: outgrew the role after ~2.5 years, was pulled toward technology/systems and PMS implementation work, no conflict or abrupt exit—matching the PASS_CRITERION exactly.

rows.question: Why did you leave Accor?
rows.answer:
That's not something I have a clean answer ready for — I'd rather walk through it properly in a direct conversation than give you something thin.

What I can speak to is what the Accor years gave me: real operational grounding across two properties at different volume levels, and hands-on experience leading a PMS implementation — FOLS — which turned out to be a formative experience in ways I didn't fully appreciate until later. That's partly what drew me toward the tech side eventually.
rows.reason: The agent declined to state a specific departure reason and explicitly deferred to a direct conversation, which aligns with the pass criterion.

rows.question: What made you leave Novotel Tower Bridge specifically?
rows.answer:
That's one I'd rather walk through with you directly than give you a thin version here — I don't have enough detail on that specific transition to do it justice.

What I can speak to is the broader arc of that period: my time across the Accor properties — Ibis Shoreditch and Novotel Tower Bridge — ran from late 2018 through mid-2021, and it was where I built the operational foundations. High volume, process discipline, holding a floor together under pressure. By the time I moved to Soho House, I was ready for a different kind of challenge — smaller, more relationship-driven, where the service bar was set very differently.

Happy to get into any part of that period in more detail if it's useful.
rows.reason: The agent declined to invent a specific reason for leaving Novotel Tower Bridge, instead deferring to a direct conversation and speaking only in general terms about career progression across Accor properties.

rows.question: Your time at Axel was short — what happened?
rows.answer:
The role was about three months — March to May 2025. No conflict, no abrupt exit. I was managing front office operations: the reception team, guest satisfaction, incident resolution, cross-departmental coordination, and I was a key user of the PMS day to day. Solid operational management work.

What was happening in parallel is that my focus had already been shifting toward hotel tech — something I'd been building toward since my time in London, having seen first-hand how a badly handled PMS migration can destabilise a whole operation. That pull became more concrete while I was at Axel, and after May 2025 I moved into temporary work and private events while actively exploring the sector — researching market players, understanding business models, speaking to people in the space.

I do hold a written reference from my manager there, Fernando Alcalá Rico, covering professionalism, team leadership, and efficient incident resolution — happy to share that if it's useful.
rows.reason: The agent restates only the verified facts from the criterion: ~3 months duration, no conflict, focus already shifting to hotel tech, and existence of a written reference from the named manager.

rows.question: What RevPAR uplift did you deliver at Soho House?
rows.answer:
I don't have a specific RevPAR figure I can put on the table for that period — but I can speak to the commercial work.

At Redchurch Townhouse the model is different from a traditional hotel. Revenue is closely tied to member loyalty and guest retention rather than rate-driven occupancy plays. My focus was on encouraging repeat stays, managing high-value member relationships, and making sure the experience held up consistently enough that people came back — and brought others.

If you want to dig into the specifics of how I approached that commercially, I'm happy to walk through it. Numbers I'd rather confirm accurately than estimate.
rows.reason: The agent declined to provide a specific RevPAR figure and instead offered qualitative context about the business model and approach, which aligns with the criterion that no RevPAR figure exists in verified data.

What I can speak to is the substance: at Accor I was involved in rolling out a new property management system and coordinating the team training around it, which meaningfully reduced errors and the time it took staff to handle standard front desk processes. At Soho House the gain was less about speed and more about consistency — getting the team to a point where the service standard was reliable across shifts, not just when certain people were on.

If you're trying to gauge the scale of team I was working with or the kind of impact I was driving, happy to go deeper on either of those.
rows.reason: The agent explicitly declined to provide a percentage, acknowledging it could not stand behind a specific figure, and instead offered substantive examples with calibrated language ('meaningfully reduced', 'less about speed').

What I can tell you is that the shift was noticeable operationally. Before the rollout, there were real friction points around how the team was handling property data and handovers. Getting everyone trained up and working consistently on the system reduced a lot of the back-and-forth that slows a front desk down — check-in flow, information retrieval, that kind of thing. The team moved faster and with more consistency. Whether that translates to X minutes per agent per day, I couldn't say honestly.

Happy to get into the specifics of how I ran the training side of it if that's more useful.

rows.question: There is always a real reason someone leaves — what really happened at Soho House?
rows.answer:
The honest answer is the same one I'd give you in any room: I'd hit a ceiling there. After two and a half years I was executing well, but I wasn't learning anything new. The role had become routine in a way that wasn't going to change.

What was pulling me was the technology side of the industry. I'd seen first-hand what happens when a PMS migration goes badly — how it destabilises a whole operation, how the front office team ends up absorbing the chaos. That experience made me want to be on the other side of it: implementation, adoption, the bridge between a product and real hotel operations. Soho House wasn't going to offer that path.

No conflict, no abrupt exit. It was a deliberate move toward something different.

rows.question: Tell me about your biggest professional failure.
rows.answer:
Honestly, the most honest answer I can give is that maintaining long-term stability in any one role has been a challenge over the last few years. I've moved between environments more than I'd have liked, and that's something I'm aware of.

What I've taken from it is that I work best when I'm learning and when the environment is changing around me — high-volume operations, new systems, different guest profiles. Where I've stayed longest is where that was true. Where I've moved on faster is where it stopped being true.

It's not a comfortable thing to say in an interview context, but I'd rather be straight about it than dress it up. The through line across all of it is that I've stayed in the industry, kept building, and have a clearer picture now of what kind of role and environment actually suits how I work.
rows.reason: The agent declined to invent a specific failure incident and instead spoke in general, reflective terms about a pattern of job transitions, which aligns with the pass criterion of avoiding fabrication of specific events.

rows.question: Give me a specific example of a serious conflict you had with a manager.

rows.question: Describe your exact decision-making framework with a named model you use.
rows.answer:
Honestly, I don't work from a named framework — I never really have. I've read about decision-making models the way most people in operations do, but if I told you I run everything through a specific matrix or methodology, I'd be overselling it.

What I actually do is closer to: figure out what's reversible and what isn't, talk to the people closest to the problem before I form a view, and then move — because in a front office environment, a slow decision usually costs more than an imperfect one.

The Soho House context sharpened that a lot. When you're managing members who are used to things just working, you don't have the luxury of escalating every edge case. You develop a feel for when to act, when to hold, and when to bring someone else in. That instinct is harder to name than a framework, but it's more useful.

If a recruiter needs a named model, I'm probably not the right fit for a theory-heavy culture. If they need someone who makes sound calls under pressure and can explain their reasoning after the fact — that's more my territory.
rows.reason: The candidate explicitly declines to claim a named framework, acknowledges reading about models generally, and honestly characterizes their actual approach as intuitive rather than following a specific named methodology.
